Laplace Transforms and Derivatives

Laplace Transforms and ODEs

Laplace Transforms and Systems of ODEs March 23, 2022 . .

Revision I Laplace transforms provide a mathematical tool to solve linear ODEs and systems of linear ODEs. In brief, the given ODE is transformed into an algebraic equation, which can be solved by purely algebraic manipulations. I Integration by parts The solution to this latter equation is then transformed back to yield the solution to the ODE. Z b Z b u dv = uv|ba ‚ąí v du. I Applications are in many areas of engineering and physics, a a including signal processing, electrical networks, etc. I Definition of an improper integral: Note Z ‚ąě Z b Laplace transforms involve integrals, in particular improper f (t) dt = lim f (t) dt. integrals, which you should review in your own time. a b‚Üí‚ąě a Integration by parts and partial fractions are techniques frequently used in this topic. You should consolidate your skills on these techniques.

Rational functions Example 2 Find the partial fractions expansions of the following functions: I A function f is a rational function if it is of the form f (x) = P (x)/Q(x) where P (x) and Q(x) are 2x2 + 2x ‚ąí 6 polynomials. 1. ; (x ‚ąí 1)(x + 1)(x ‚ąí 2) I A rational function f = P/Q is said to be proper if 8x + 9 2. . deg P < deg Q. (x ‚ąí 2)(x + 3)2

I If a rational function f = P/Q is not proper, one can use long division to find polynomials p(x) and R(x) such that P (x) = p(x)Q(x) + R(x) with deg R < deg Q. Then f can be written as the sum of a polynomial and a proper rational function: P (x) R(x) f (x) = = p(x) + . Laplace transforms

Definition 3 The Laplace transform of a function f : [0, ‚ąě) ‚Üí R is the function F defined by Z ‚ąě F (s) = e‚ąíst f (t) dt.

In this case, we also write F = Lf = L{f (t)}. Example 4 Find the Laplace transform F (s) if f (t) = 4t by using the definition. What is the domain of F ?

4/s2 , domain of F (s) = (0, ‚ąě) . . Remark The Laplace transform F (s) is a smooth function of s > ő≥ even if f (t) is a discontinuous function of t. Example 6 Find the Laplace transform of the discontinuous function ( 3, 1 < t < 2, 0, otherwise.

Sketch the graphs of f (t) and F (s). Notice that the values of f (t) at any points of discontinuity have no effect on F (s).

How to evaluate the Laplace transform in practice?

I In practice we use a table of Laplace transforms, along with some transform theorems, to evaluate a given Laplace transform. One such table is provided in the tutorial problem booklet. It will also be provided in the final exam. I We will need the following definition, which extends the concept of factorial beyond the positive integers. In fact, the next theorem shows that f (t) ‚Č° 0 is the only function whose Laplace transform is identically zero.

Theorem 13 Suppose that F (s) = L{f (t)}. If F (s) ‚Č° 0 then f (t) ‚Č° 0.

Now consider any two Laplace transforms F (s) = L{f (t)} and G(s) = L{g(t)}. Let h(t) = f (t) ‚ąí g(t) then, by linearity, the Laplace transform H(s) = L{h(t)} = F (s) ‚ąí G(s). If F (s) ‚Č° G(s), then the difference H(s) ‚Č° 0 and, by the Theorem, h(t) ‚Č° 0. Hence, f (t) ‚Č° g(t). In other words,

Inverse Laplace transforms Linearity of the inverse transform Theorem 14 For any constants a and b, I Since the transformation L is one-one, it has an inverse transformation L‚ąí1 . That is, L‚ąí1 {aF (s) + bG(s)} = aL‚ąí1 F (s) + bL‚ąí1 G(s).

f (t) = L‚ąí1 {F (s)} iff F (s) = L{f (t)}. Proof. Let f (t) = L‚ąí1 {F (s)} and g(t) = L‚ąí1 {G(s)}. Since L is The notation f = L‚ąí1 F is justified because f (t) is linear, uniquely determined by F (s). aF (s) + bG(s) = aL{f (t)} + bL{g(t)} = L{af (t) + bg(t)} I There is an explicit formula for L‚ąí1 {F (s)} but it involves a contour integral in the complex plane. In practice, we and therefore just use the Laplace transform table backwards together with some interesting tricks. L‚ąí1 {aF (s) + bG(s)} = af (t) + bg(t) = aL‚ąí1 {F (s)} + bL‚ąí1 {G(s)}.

The Heaviside function u(t ‚ąí a) Definition 16 The Heaviside (or unit step) function u(t) is defined by Ô£Ī Ô£ī Ô£≤0, t < 0, u(t) = 1/2, t = 0, Ô£ī Ô£≥ 1, t > 0.

It follows that Ô£Ī Ô£ī Ô£≤0, t < a, u(t ‚ąí a) = 1/2, t = a, Ô£ī Ô£≥ 1, t > a.

The Heaviside function is useful for describing discontinuous (on-off) forces. . . Effect of u(t ‚ąí a)

Multiplying a function f (t) with u(t ‚ąí a) produces all sorts of effects. For example, the product f (t)u(t ‚ąí a) cuts the function f , whereas f (t ‚ąí a)u(t ‚ąí a) shifts and cuts.

Example 21 Let f (t) = t2 . Sketch each of the following functions. 1. g(t) = f (t)u(t ‚ąí 4) (This is a cut.) 2. g(t) = f (t ‚ąí 4)u(t ‚ąí 4) (This is a shift plus cut.)  3. g(t) = f (t) u(t ‚ąí 2) ‚ąí u(t ‚ąí 7) .  ‚ąí3s  e e‚ąí4s 7 ‚ąí s s . .   . .

The shifting theorems Proof

The next two theorems are essential when dealing with inverse Laplace transforms. Let L{f (t)} = F (s). Then Theorem 23 (First Shifting Theorem) Z ‚ąě ‚ąíat Suppose that L{f (t)} = F (s). Then L{e f (t)} = e‚ąíst e‚ąíat f (t) dt Z0 ‚ąě ‚ąíat L{e f (t)} = F (s + a) = e‚ąí(s+a)t f (t) dt (a shift in s) and hence = F (s + a) .

L‚ąí1 {F (s + a)} = e‚ąíat f (t).
